result.markdown:
# Quarto and R {#sec-r}

This chapter covers R-specific features when working with Quarto.
When you include R code cells in your document, Quarto automatically uses the knitr engine to execute them.
The basics of code cells, including syntax and common options like `echo` and `include`, are covered in @sec-code.
Here you'll learn about features specific to R and the knitr engine.

## Output {#sec-r-output}

This section covers the various ways R code produces output in Quarto documents---from inline values and data frames to figures and interactive widgets.

### Inline code {#sec-inline-code}

In addition to code cells, you can embed R code directly within your markdown text using inline code.
This is useful for inserting computed values into your narrative without manually copying them.

The syntax uses single backticks with `r` after the opening backtick:

```markdown
The mean temperature was `{{r}} mean(airquality$Temp)` degrees.
```

Typically, you'll compute values in a code cell and then reference them inline:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
avg_temp <- mean(airquality$Temp)
```
:::


```markdown
The mean temperature was `{{r}} avg_temp` degrees.
```

Which renders as: The mean temperature was 77.8823529 degrees.

::: callout-warning
## Keep inline code simple

Inline expressions should be confined to simple values that you have pre-computed in code cells.
Avoid complex function calls or operations that do significant work.
:::

By default, inline code produces plain text.
To generate markdown-formatted output, wrap the result in `I()`:

```markdown
The result is `{{r}} I("**significant**")`.
```

This produces bold text in the rendered output.

::: callout-note
## Inline code in YAML

Inline code cannot be used directly in YAML strings like titles or figure captions.
For dynamic captions, use the cross-reference div syntax instead (see @sec-crossreferences).
:::

### Data frame printing {#sec-df-print}

Quarto provides the `df-print` option to control how data frames are displayed in your output.
This is particularly useful in HTML documents where you can enable interactive tables.

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `default` | Use the standard S3 print method |
| `kable` | Render as a markdown table using `knitr::kable()` |
| `tibble` | Print as plain text tibble |
| `paged` | Create an interactive HTML table with pagination |

Set this option in your document's YAML header:

```yaml
---
title: "My Analysis"
format:
  html:
    df-print: paged
---
```

The `paged` option is especially useful for exploring data interactively, as it allows readers to page through rows and search within the table.

### Graphics devices {#sec-graphics-devices}

When R generates a plot, knitr uses a *graphics device* to save it to a file, which is then included in the output.
The default device is `png`, which works well in most situations.
However, you may want to change it for better text rendering, scalable output, or PDF compatibility.

#### Choosing a device

Set the graphics device with the `dev` option, either at the document level or for individual cells:

```yaml
---
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: ragg_png
---
```

Or for a single cell:

```{{r}}
#| dev: svglite
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()
```

Common choices include:

| Device | Best for | Notes |
|--------|----------|-------|
| `png` | General use (default) | Raster; fast and widely supported |
| `ragg_png` | Higher-quality PNG | Better text rendering and anti-aliasing; requires the **ragg** package |
| `svglite` | Scalable HTML output | Vector format; sharp at any zoom level; requires the **svglite** package |
| `cairo_pdf` | PDF with Unicode text | Handles non-Latin characters and special symbols |
| `pdf` | Standard PDF figures | Default vector device for LaTeX/PDF output |

::: callout-tip
## Automatic device selection for PDF

The R option `knitr.graphics.auto_pdf` (shown in @sec-execution-options-r) tells knitr to automatically use a PDF graphics device when rendering to PDF output and a PNG device otherwise.
This saves you from having to set `dev` differently for each format.
:::

#### The `fig-format` option

Quarto also provides a `fig-format` option that works across engines (not just knitr):

```yaml
---
format:
  html:
    fig-format: svg
---
```

This is a simpler alternative when you just need to change the output file format without customizing the specific graphics device.
When both `dev` and `fig-format` are set, the `dev` option takes precedence.

### Multiple figures from a code cell {#sec-multiple-figures}

A single R code cell can produce multiple plots.
By default, each plot appears as a separate figure in the output.
You can arrange them as labeled subfigures using `fig-cap` and `fig-subcap`.

#### Subfigure captions

Provide `fig-cap` as the overall caption and `fig-subcap` as a list of individual subcaptions:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-mtcars-panels
#| fig-cap: "Motor Trend car data"
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Weight vs. fuel efficiency"
#|   - "Displacement vs. fuel efficiency"
#| layout-ncol: 2
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = disp, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()
```

This produces two side-by-side subfigures, each with its own caption, under a shared parent caption.
The individual panels are cross-referenceable as `@fig-mtcars-panels-1` and `@fig-mtcars-panels-2`, while `@fig-mtcars-panels` refers to the group.

#### Layout options

The `layout-ncol` option controls how many columns the subfigures occupy.
For more complex arrangements, use the `layout` option to specify custom grids:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-custom-layout
#| fig-cap: "Custom layout"
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Plot A"
#|   - "Plot B"
#|   - "Plot C"
#| layout: [[1,1], [1]]

plot(1:10)
plot(10:1)
plot(1:20)
```

The `layout: [[1,1], [1]]` specification places the first two plots side by side in the first row, with the third plot spanning the full width of the second row.
For a full explanation of the layout syntax, see @sec-layout.

### Interactive output {#sec-interactive-output-r}

R has a rich ecosystem of packages that produce interactive HTML output.
These packages---collectively known as [htmlwidgets](https://www.htmlwidgets.org/)---work in Quarto documents without any special configuration.
Just load the package and call the function as you would in an interactive R session.

#### Common htmlwidgets packages

**Interactive plots with plotly:**

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point()

ggplotly(p)
```

**Interactive tables with DT:**

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
DT::datatable(mtcars, options = list(pageLength = 5))
```

**Interactive maps with leaflet:**

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
library(leaflet)

leaflet() |>
  addTiles() |>
  addMarkers(lng = -73.99, lat = 40.73, popup = "New York City")
```

Other popular htmlwidgets packages include **dygraphs** for time series, **visNetwork** for network graphs, and **reactable** for feature-rich tables.

::: callout-warning
## HTML-only

Interactive widgets only work in HTML output formats.
When rendering to PDF or Word, widgets will either produce a static fallback image or an error, depending on the package.
Use `knitr::is_html_output()` (see @sec-conditional-execution) to conditionally include widgets only in HTML output.
:::

::: callout-note
## Widget dependencies and self-contained documents

Each htmlwidget brings its own JavaScript and CSS dependencies.
In self-contained HTML documents (`embed-resources: true`), these dependencies are bundled into the file, which can increase its size.
If your document uses many different widget packages, consider using the default non-self-contained output and hosting the result as a directory.
:::

## Configuration {#sec-r-configuration}

This section covers options that control how R code cells are executed and how their behavior can vary across documents and output formats.

### Execution options {#sec-execution-options-r}

@sec-code introduced cell-level options like `echo` and `include`.
Many of these options can also be set at the document level to apply to all code cells.

#### Document-level options

Set execution options for the entire document using the `execute` key in your YAML header:

```yaml
---
title: "My Report"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---
```

Common document-level execution options include:

- `eval`: Whether to execute code cells
- `echo`: Whether to display source code
- `output`: Whether to include results (`true`, `false`, or `asis`)
- `warning`: Whether to include warnings
- `error`: Whether to include errors (without halting)
- `include`: Whether to include any output at all
- `fig-width` and `fig-height`: Default figure dimensions in inches

#### Knitr-specific options

The knitr engine has additional options that you can set using the `knitr` key.
These map directly to knitr's chunk options:

```yaml
---
title: "My Document"
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    collapse: true
    comment: "#>"
    R.options:
      knitr.graphics.auto_pdf: true
---
```

The `R.options` setting is particularly useful---it temporarily sets R options via `options()` before each code cell executes, then restores the original values afterward.

#### Cell-level options

Individual code cells can override document-level settings.
The knitr engine supports a large set of cell-level options.
Some commonly used ones include:

**Code appearance:**

- `code-fold`: Collapse code into an expandable section
- `code-summary`: Text shown for collapsed code (e.g., "Show code")
- `code-line-numbers`: Add line numbers to code

**Output control:**

- `results`: How to display text results (`markup`, `asis`, `hold`, `hide`)
- `collapse`: Merge code and output into a single block
- `comment`: Prefix for output lines (default `"##"`)

**Figures:**

- `fig-cap`: Figure caption
- `fig-alt`: Alternative text for accessibility
- `fig-align`: Alignment (`left`, `center`, `right`)
- `fig-dpi`: Resolution in dots per inch
- `out-width`, `out-height`: Output dimensions

**Caching:**

- `cache`: Enable caching for the cell
- `dependson`: Specify cells this one depends on

For a complete list of knitr cell options, see the [Quarto reference](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-knitr.html).

#### Raw markdown output

Sometimes you want your R code to produce raw markdown that Quarto processes directly, rather than being wrapped in output containers.
Use `output: asis` for this:

```{{r}}
#| output: asis
cat("## A Dynamic Heading\n\n")
cat("This paragraph was generated by R.\n")
```

This is useful for programmatically generating document structure, such as creating sections in a loop.

### Parameters {#sec-parameters}

Parameters let you create variations of a document without editing the source.
This is useful for generating reports for different regions, time periods, or scenarios.

#### Defining parameters

Define parameters in your YAML header using the `params` key:

```yaml
---
title: "Regional Sales Report"
params:
  region: "North"
  year: 2024
  show_details: true
---
```

#### Using parameters in code

Access parameters through the `params` list:

```{{r}}
#| echo: false
library(dplyr)

sales_data |>
  filter(region == params$region, year == params$year)
```

```markdown
This report covers the `{{r}} params$region` region for `{{r}} params$year`.
```

#### Rendering with different parameter values

Override parameters from the command line using the `-P` flag:

```bash
quarto render report.qmd -P region:South -P year:2025
```

For multiple parameter sets, create a YAML file with the values:

```{.yaml filename="params-south.yml"}
region: "South"
year: 2025
show_details: false
```

Then reference it when rendering:

```bash
quarto render report.qmd --execute-params params-south.yml
```

### Conditional execution {#sec-conditional-execution}

Sometimes you need code to behave differently depending on the output format.
Quarto provides several ways to access format information from your R code.

#### Using knitr functions

The knitr package provides functions to check the output format:

```{{r}}
if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
 # Code for HTML output only
}

if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
  # Code for PDF output only
}
```

You can also check the specific Pandoc output format:

```{{r}}
knitr::pandoc_to()
# Returns e.g., "html", "latex", "docx"
```

#### Using Quarto environment variables

For more detailed format information, Quarto sets the `QUARTO_EXECUTE_INFO` environment variable during rendering:

```{{r}}
info <- jsonlite::fromJSON(Sys.getenv("QUARTO_EXECUTE_INFO"))
format_name <- info$format$identifier$`base-format`

if (format_name == "html") {
  # HTML-specific code
}
```

This approach gives you access to additional metadata about the rendering context.

## Rendering {#sec-r-rendering}

This section covers ways to invoke Quarto rendering from within R and how to render R scripts directly.

### Rendering from R {#sec-render-from-r}

The **quarto** R package provides functions to render documents directly from R, without using the terminal.

#### Installation

```r
install.packages("quarto")
```

Note that this package is optional---you don't need it to use Quarto with R.
It's a convenience for those who prefer working entirely within the R console.

#### Basic rendering

Use `quarto_render()` to render a document:

```r
library(quarto)

quarto_render("report.qmd")
quarto_render("report.qmd", output_format = "pdf")
```

#### Preview

For iterative work, `quarto_preview()` starts a live preview that updates as you save changes:

```r
quarto_preview("report.qmd")

# When done:
quarto_preview_stop()
```

#### Rendering with parameters

Pass parameters using the `execute_params` argument:

```r
quarto_render(
  "report.qmd",
  execute_params = list(region = "South", year = 2025)
)
```

### Rendering R scripts {#sec-render-scripts}

You can render R scripts (`.R` files) directly with Quarto, without converting them to `.qmd` files first.
This uses syntax based on `knitr::spin()`.

#### Script syntax

- Lines starting with `#'` are treated as markdown
- Lines starting with `#|` are code cell options
- All other lines are R code

Here's an example script:

```{.r filename="analysis.R"}
#' ---
#' title: "Penguin Analysis"
#' author: "Your Name"
#' format: html
#' ---

#' ## Setup

library(palmerpenguins)
library(ggplot2)

#' ## Bill Size Analysis
#'
#' @fig-bill-sizes shows the relationship between
#' bill length and depth.

#| label: fig-bill-sizes
#| fig-cap: "Bill dimensions by species"
#| warning: false

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()
```

#### Rendering scripts

Render the script just like a `.qmd` file:

```bash
quarto render analysis.R
quarto preview analysis.R
```

This approach is useful when you want to keep your analysis as a plain R script that can also be sourced or run interactively.

## Environment {#sec-r-environment}

This section covers how Quarto interacts with your R installation and how to troubleshoot issues that arise from environment differences.

### How Quarto finds R {#sec-quarto-finds-r}

Quarto looks for an R installation in the following order:

1. The `QUARTO_R` environment variable, if set
2. The R executable found on your system `PATH`
3. Platform-specific default locations (e.g., `/usr/local/bin/R` on macOS, the Windows registry)

If Quarto is using a different R than you expect, you can override the lookup by setting `QUARTO_R` to the path of your preferred R binary.
One way to do this is in a `.env` file in your project directory:

```{.bash filename=".env"}
QUARTO_R=/opt/R/4.4.0/bin/R
```

Quarto automatically reads `.env` files in the project root during rendering.

To see which R installation Quarto is currently using, run `quarto check` from the terminal:

```bash
quarto check knitr
```

The output includes the detected R version and path, along with the knitr and rmarkdown package versions.
If the R path shown doesn't match what you expect, that's likely the source of any "package not found" or version mismatch issues.

### Diagnosing environment issues {#sec-diagnosing-env}

When Quarto renders a document, it starts a fresh R session.
This session might use a different R installation or library paths than what you use interactively.

Create a code cell that outputs your session information:

```{{r}}
sessionInfo()
.libPaths()
```

For more detailed diagnostics, use the **sessioninfo** package:

```{{r}}
#| eval: false
sessioninfo::session_info(to_file = "quarto-session-info.txt")
```

Then compare this output with your interactive R session to identify discrepancies.

### Common issues {#sec-common-issues-r}

- **Different R version**: Quarto may find a different R installation than expected
- **Missing packages**: The library paths Quarto uses may not include packages you've installed
- **PATH issues**: Environment variables may differ between your shell and Quarto's execution environment

### Using multiple languages {#sec-multiple-languages}

While this chapter focuses on R, Quarto documents can include code cells from multiple languages.
Each language runs in its own separate session---objects created in R are not directly accessible from Python, and vice versa.

```{{r}}
x <- 42
```

```{{python}}
# This cannot access the R variable x
y = 100
```

This is useful when you want to use the strengths of different languages in the same document.
For example, you might use R for statistical modeling and Python for certain visualizations.

::: callout-tip
## Sharing data between languages

To share data between R and Python cells, save intermediate results to files (e.g., CSV, Parquet) and read them in the other language.
For tighter integration, consider the **reticulate** package, which allows R and Python to share objects directly.
:::

If your document contains only R code cells, Quarto uses the knitr engine.
If it contains only Python cells, it uses Jupyter.
When both are present, Quarto uses the engine specified in your YAML header, or knitr by default.
